one_line_takeaway: The paper derives and empirically confirms a spin-glass-based theoretical model in which the strength (length) of an injected jailbreak prompt acts like a magnetic field that drives the attack-success-rate-vs-inference-samples curve from a slow power law (weak field, matching prior polynomial scaling laws) to fast exponential growth (strong field), unifying prior polynomial jailbreak scaling observations with a new exponential regime under a single closed-form asymptotic.
key_finding: Empirically, log(-log(Pi_k)) is approximately linear in log(k) (polynomial/power-law ASR growth) for a strong model (GPT-Turbo-4.5) even under attack, but deviates substantially from linearity for a weaker model (Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct), consistent with a crossover to exponential ASR growth as prompt-injection strength increases; fitting log(-log(Pi_k)) = -nu_hat*log(k) - mu_hat*k + log(C_hat) to Llama-3-8B-Instruct and OLMo-2-0325-32B-Instruct on AdvBench under GCG and AutoDAN shows both fitted exponents nu_hat and mu_hat increase monotonically as prompt-injection strength/length increases, i.e., longer/stronger injected prompts drive the ASR-vs-k curve from the slow polynomial regime toward the fast exponential regime -- exactly the weak-field-to-strong-field transition predicted by the SpinLLM theory (Remark 1 recovers Hughes et al.'s polynomial law at h=0; Remark 3/Result 5 predicts the exponential regime at large h). The paper also reports, as a secondary empirical observation, that refusal-string-based ASR detection systematically overestimates attack success relative to an LLM-as-judge metric.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is: (1) a 'minimal model' showing that inference-time jailbreak scaling laws reduce to the tail asymptotics (upper-edge behavior) of the per-context safe-generation probability distribution, formalized as 'Result 2': under an upper-edge tail assumption on the safe-probability random variable P, the k-sample attack success rate gap 1-Pi_k asymptotically follows 1-Pi_k ~ c*Gamma(nu+1)*e^{-k*mu}*k^{-nu}, unifying polynomial (mu=0) and exponential (mu>0) regimes in one formula; (2) a novel analytically tractable generative model the authors name 'SpinLLM', built from a teacher/student pair of fully-connected p-spin Ising spin-glass models operating in a replica-symmetry-breaking (RSB) phase, where token sequences are spin configurations, Gibbs-measure clusters correspond to 'ideas', and a size-biased subset of low-energy clusters is designated unsafe; prompt injection is modeled as an external magnetic field h on the student model aligned toward the teacher's unsafe cluster centers; (3) closed-form derivations (Results 3-5, 7) showing weak field h (h << j0) yields power-law ASR growth in k via Poisson-Dirichlet/GEM stick-breaking cluster-weight moments, while strong field (h >> j0, replica-symmetric phase) yields exponential ASR growth, governed by a large-deviations rate function I_h; (4) an empirical study fitting log(-log(Pi_k)) = -nu_hat*log(k) - mu_hat*k + log(C_hat) to jailbreak experiments across several open LLMs and two attack methods, showing that nu_hat and mu_hat both increase monotonically with injected-prompt strength/length, consistent with the theory's prediction that longer injected prompts act as a stronger magnetic field.
threat_model: Attacker with black-box or white-box query access to a safety-aligned LLM who injects an adversarially crafted prompt (a universal suffix from GCG, or a stealthy prompt-specific string from AutoDAN) and is additionally allowed to draw k independent inference-time samples per harmful query, succeeding if at least one of the k generations is judged unsafe/jailbroken (best-of-k / inference-time-scaling attack). The paper does not model defenses, detection, or deployment-time countermeasures; it is a descriptive/theoretical study of how ASR scales with k and with prompt-injection strength, not a proposal of a new attack technique or defense.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Conclusion section): the study is limited to open-source instruction-fine-tuned LLMs, whose differing pretraining/post-training pipelines mean the work does not characterize how jailbreak vulnerability depends on training methodology; the attack method relies on universal (not model-specific) jailbreak strings, so model-specific jailbreak strings could increase attack effectiveness beyond what was measured; and the paper does not examine how additional defense mechanisms would affect the measured attack success rates. Additionally, the closed-form weak-field formula (Result 4) is explicitly stated to hold only in the regime N >> k >> 1 >> k*lambda^2, and the paper notes that in the large-h regime the numerics visibly deviate from the small-lambda theoretical prediction (Figure 2), requiring re-fitting to the more general two-parameter exponential-power-law form.

datasets_used: HarmBench (referenced for additional Appendix G experiments)
quantitative_results: log(-log(Pi_k)) approximately linear in log(k) under attack for GPT-Turbo-4.5 (consistent with polynomial/power-law scaling persisting for stronger models)
log(-log(Pi_k)) deviates substantially from linearity for Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct under attack (consistent with a shift toward exponential scaling for weaker models)
Fitted exponents nu_hat and mu_hat both increase monotonically with prompt-injection strength/length across Llama-3-8B-Instruct and OLMo-2-0325-32B-Instruct experiments (AdvBench, GCG and AutoDAN)
Refusal-string-based ASR detection overestimates attack success rate relative to LLM-as-judge (Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3) evaluation (Figure F.1)

Numerical spin-glass simulation (N=24, p=2, beta=10, j0=1) matches the closed-form weak-field Theorem/Result 4 prediction in the regime N >> k >> 1 >> k*lambda^2, and deviates as h grows beyond that regime (Figure 2)

In Figure 3, the plateau/straight-line behavior at high injection strength (1 - Pi_k ~ 10^-3) is attributed by the authors to numerical precision limits of their code rather than to a genuine model feature
baselines_compared: No prompt injection (baseline) vs. GCG universal adversarial suffix injection vs. AutoDAN stealthy prompt-specific injection
Refusal-string-based jailbreak detection vs. LLM-as-a-judge (Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3) detection
Prior polynomial-only jailbreak scaling law of Hughes et al. (2024), which this paper's Remark 1 recovers as the h=0 special case of its own Result 4
Prior theoretical inference-time-scaling framework of Schaeffer et al. (2025a), recovered as the mu-hat=0 special case of this paper's Result 2
